title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Life Insurance and Life Assurance: Know the Simple Difference
The difference between life insurance and life assurance is not always clear to everyone. Many people think these two words mean the same thing, but they do not. Both are types of life cover that give money to your loved ones when you are no longer around. But the way they work is a bit different.
In life insurance, you are covered for a fixed time, like 10, 20, or 30 years. If you pass away during this time, your family gets the money. If you live longer than the policy term, the cover ends, and no money is paid.
In life assurance, you are covered for your whole life. No matter when you die, the money will be paid to your family. Because of this lifetime cover, it usually costs more than life insurance.
Let’s understand each one in detail and see how they are different.
What is Life Insurance?
Life insurance is a policy that protects your family if something happens to you during a fixed period. It is also called term insurance.
Here is how life insurance works:
You choose a policy term, for example, 20 years.
You pay a small amount of money every month. This is called the premium.
If you die during the term of 20 years, your family will receive a fixed amount of money. This is called the sum assured.
If you are still alive at the end of 20 years, the policy ends, and you don’t get any money back.
There are 3 types of life insurance:
Level Term Insurance – The cover amount stays the same throughout the term.
Increasing Term Insurance – The cover amount increases over time, usually to match inflation.
Decreasing Term Insurance – The cover amount reduces over time, mostly used with home loans.
Life insurance is helpful if you want to:
Protect your family while your children are still young.
Cover your home loan or other big loans.
Make sure your loved ones have money if you pass away early.
But remember, life insurance only works during the policy term. After that, the cover ends.
What is Life Assurance?
Life assurance is a plan that covers you for your whole life. That means it pays out no matter when you die. Because a payout is guaranteed, it is often called whole life cover.
Let’s understand this better with these simple points:
You pay regular premiums, just like in life insurance.
But there is no policy term – the cover lasts for your entire life.
When you die, the money is given to your family.
Your cover does not end until you pass away.
Some life assurance plans also grow in value over time.
Key features of life assurance:
Lifelong cover: Your family is sure to receive a payout.
Fixed or increasing premiums: You may pay the same or higher premiums based on your plan.
Higher cost: Because the cover lasts forever, premiums are usually higher than term life insurance.
No decrease: Cover does not reduce over time like decreasing term plans.
Life assurance is often chosen by people who want to:
Leave behind a gift or inheritance for their loved ones.
Cover funeral or medical costs.
Have peace of mind that their family will get money no matter when they die.
Which is Best for Me? – Life Insurance vs Life Assurance?
Choosing between life insurance vs life assurance depends on your needs, age, and future plans. Below are a few simple points that can help you decide what may suit you better:
You may prefer life insurance if:
You only need coverage while your children are young or while your loan is unpaid.
You want lower premiums.
You are looking for short to medium-term protection.
You may prefer life assurance if:
You want your family to get money no matter when you die.
You want to cover funeral or final medical expenses.
You are thinking of long-term wealth planning.
Still unsure? You can always speak to a financial expert who can explain what’s best based on your personal life and money matters.
Conclusion
It is important to know the difference between life insurance and life assurance so you can choose what suits your needs best. While both give your family financial help when you are not around, they work in different ways. Life insurance gives protection for a limited time, usually for a set term like 10 or 20 years. If you survive that term, your cover ends.
Life assurance, on the other hand, gives you peace of mind forever. It stays active till the end of your life, and your family will surely get the payout. This is why premiums are usually higher for life assurance.
In the comparison of life assurance vs life insurance, there is no right or wrong choice. It depends on what you need and can afford. If you are young and need cover for a short period, life insurance may be better. But if you are planning for the long-term and want to leave something behind, life assurance could be a good fit.
Choose wisely and consider your family’s needs, loans, and future plans. If in doubt, a financial expert can help you decide the right option.
FAQs
What is life insurance and life assurance?
The difference between life insurance and life assurance is mainly about the policy term. Life insurance covers you for a fixed period. If you die during that time, your family gets a payout. If you outlive the term, nothing is paid. Life assurance, on the other hand, gives a payout whenever you die, because it covers you for your whole life.
Is assurance the same as insurance?
Insurance means protection for a fixed time. If an event happens during that time (like death), a payout is made. Assurance means the event is certain to happen (like death), and the payout is guaranteed. So, life insurance is time-based, and life assurance is life-long.
Does life assurance cover the same things as general insurance?
Life assurance is for your life and pays out after you die. It covers the whole life and has a guaranteed payout. General insurance is for things like health, car, or travel. It only pays if an event (like an accident or illness) happens.
What does life insurance cover, and how does reinsurance work?
People obtain life insurance to provide financial protection for their families in case they pass away. Reinsurance is the insurance for the insurers. It enables insurance companies to transfer a part of their risk to another insurance company so that the risk can be managed and losses mitigated.
